preFix.title: Composting coffee grounds and tea leaves
preFix.subtitle: How these daily kitchen additions affect the heap and how to use them correctly
preFix.excerpt: Coffee grounds and loose tea leaves are greens, nitrogen-rich additions with a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio around 20:1 for coffee and 15:1 for tea. Both compost quickly in a warm, active heap and add trace minerals. The common mistake is adding too much coffee at once, creating a dense mat that goes anaerobic. Used in moderation and mixed through the heap, both are consistently useful additions rather than something to feel virtuous about.
preFix.sourceNotes: Oregon State University Extension Service guidance on composting coffee grounds. RHS composting guidance 2024. Industry guidance on coffee as compost activator.

content.text: Coffee grounds are brown in colour but green in composting terms: their

of around 20:1 puts them in the same category as fresh grass clippings. They also contain small quantities of magnesium, copper, and potassium. A household that produces one mug of coffee per day generates approximately 7 g of grounds, small enough not to matter on its own, but worth directing to the heap rather than the bin.

Add coffee grounds directly to the compost heap or caddy in small amounts with each addition. Mix them through the top layer of the heap rather than leaving them as a lump, coffee grounds pack densely when wet and can create an anaerobic pocket if added in a thick layer. A typical day's grounds from one household (7 to 15 g) mixed through the active layer presents no problem. A batch from a café (several kilograms) needs to be mixed with a large volume of carbon material.

Unbleached paper coffee filters compost well, they have a high cellulose content and break down within weeks. Bleached white filters (chlorine or oxygen-bleached) are also compostable; the bleaching treatment leaves no residue that causes problems at home-composting temperatures. Add the filter with the grounds still inside it.
Tea bags and loose leaf tea

Loose leaf tea goes directly into the heap or caddy. Tea bags are more complex: most mainstream tea bags contain a small amount of polypropylene mesh to seal them, typically 0.5 to 1 g per bag, which does not biodegrade and will appear in the finished compost as a small piece of plastic film. Bags labelled 'plant-based', 'biodegradable', or 'plastic-free' use a corn-starch or abaca-fibre mesh and compost completely. If your tea brand is not confirmed plastic-free, empty the bag and compost the leaf material only, or switch to loose leaf.
Effect on heap pH and worms

Coffee grounds have a pH of around 6.2 when fresh. The claim that they acidify compost or soil significantly is not supported by the data, once composted, they are near-neutral. Worms do eat coffee grounds actively; the coarseness of the particles provides grinding material in their gut. An excess of grounds relative to other materials does reduce earthworm activity (too dense a texture), but a 5 to 10% coffee content by volume in an active heap presents no problem.
